coredata.dc:title: Controlled synthesis of platinum nanoflowers supported on carbon quantum dots as a highly effective catalyst for methanol electro-oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Sha, Rinky
Solomon Jones, S.
Badhulika, Sushmee
coredata.dc:description: This work reports a platinum (Pt) nanoflowers-carbon quantum dots (CQD) composite based electro-catalyst for ultra-high performance methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) wherein CQD was prepared by a novel, single-step, inexpensive, environmental-friendly pyrolysis method followed by electro-deposition of Pt nanoflowers homogeneously distributed over CQD modified electrode for excellent electrocatalytic properties and optimum usage of Pt nanostructures. The Pt-CQD composite displayed a significantly enhanced electrocatalytic activity, excellent stability, lower onset potential (0.26 V) with CO tolerance ability (If/Ib = 5.58) which is, to the best of authors' knowledge, ~2.1 times greater than other earlier reported carbon nanomaterials supported catalysts. Values of If and If/Ib of Pt-CQD composite is 5.28 and 2.04 folds higher than only Pt which is ascribed to its greater electrochemical surface area because of the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups in CQD, the synergistic effects of CQD and Pt and excellent utilization of Pt nanoflowers on CQD support. Thus, this binder-free composite exhibits huge potential to be used as an economical, effective and stable electro-catalyst for MOR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Platinum nanoflowers
Carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Methanol oxidation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphic signifying fabrication steps of Pt-CQD based electrode.
TEM images of only CQD (a) lower and (b) higher magnification respectively.

FESEM images of Pt (5 cycles)-CQD composite at (a) low; (b) high magnification; Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite at (c) low; (d) high magnification and Pt (15 cycles)-CQD composite at (e) low; (f) high magnification.
EDX spectra of (a) Pt(5 cycles)-CQD composite; (b) Pt(10 cycles)-CQD composite; (c) Pt(15 cycles)-CQD composite; (d–f) Elemental mapping of Pt(10 cycles)-CQD composite.

(A) Overlay plot of C 1s spectra for Pt (5, 10,15 cycles)-CQD composites; (B) Overlay plot of O 1s spectra of Pt (5, 10,15 cycles)-CQD composites; (C) Overlay plot of Pt 4f spectra of Pt (5, 10,15 cycles)-CQD composites; (D) Overlay plot of the entire survey spectrum corresponding to all Pt-CQD composites and (E–G) Deconvoluted C 1s spectra, O 1s spectra and Pt 4f spectra for Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite respectively.
XRD patterns of all Pt-CQD composites.
CVs of the Pt-CQD composites in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte at the scan rate of 50 mV/s.

CVs of (a) Pt(5 cycles)-CQD, (b)Pt(10 cycles)-CQD, (c) Pt(15 cycles)-CQD composites and (d) a comparative study on the electrochemical behaviors of Pt(10 cycles)-CQD composite and pristine Pt (10 cycles) modified GCE with the commercial Pt-C modified GCE in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte and 0.5 M methanol at the scan rate of 50 mV/s.

Graph representing a linear relationship between the forward peak currents vs. square root of the scan rates ranging from 50 to 200 mV/s on the surface of Pt(10 cycles)-CQD/GCE in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte and 0.5 M methanol.
Chronoamperometry plots of all Pt-CQD composites and commercial Pt-C based electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte and 0.5 M methanol for 3000 s at the oxidation potential of 0.6 V.
A comparative study on the electrocatalytic activities of only Pt, all Pt-CQD composites based electrodes towards MOR.
A comparative study on the performance of Pt(10 cycles)-CQD catalyst towards methanol oxidation.

row.entry:
12. Pt nanoflowers decorated rGO k -SnO2
m composite

head.ce:title: Controlled synthesis of platinum nanoflowers supported on carbon quantum dots as a highly effective catalyst for methanol electro-oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Hyderabad 502285, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports a platinum (Pt) nanoflowers-carbon quantum dots (CQD) composite based electro-catalyst for ultra-high performance methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) wherein CQD was prepared by a novel, single-step, inexpensive, environmental-friendly pyrolysis method followed by electro-deposition of Pt nanoflowers homogeneously distributed over CQD modified electrode for excellent electrocatalytic properties and optimum usage of Pt nanostructures. The Pt-CQD composite displayed a significantly enhanced electrocatalytic activity, excellent stability, lower onset potential (0.26 V) with CO tolerance ability (If/Ib = 5.58) which is, to the best of authors' knowledge, ~2.1 times greater than other earlier reported carbon nanomaterials supported catalysts. Values of If and If/Ib of Pt-CQD composite is 5.28 and 2.04 folds higher than only Pt which is ascribed to its greater electrochemical surface area because of the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups in CQD, the synergistic effects of CQD and Pt and excellent utilization of Pt nanoflowers on CQD support. Thus, this binder-free composite exhibits huge potential to be used as an economical, effective and stable electro-catalyst for MOR.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid depletion of fossil fuels and the descending environmental pollution problems necessitates the use of sustainable and clean energy sources. In this regard, direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) have attracted significant interest as a potential green power source used for multi-functional electronic devices and vehicles because of high energy efficiency, simple fabrication process, easiness in refueling or storage of liquid fuel, wide operating temperature (25–120 °C), low pollution emission [1–3]. Nevertheless, the commercialization of DMFCs is still hindered by numerous factors like inferior electrocatalytic activity, durability and excessive cost of catalysts for the methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) [4,5]. Hence, preparation of the cost-effective catalyst with excellent electrocatalytic efficacy and durability is a field of research of utmost significance.

Platinum (Pt) has been widely utilized as a catalyst in MOR since it effectively oxidizes methanol. The mechanism of DMFCs lies in the oxidation of methanol to carbon dioxide at the surface of the catalyst modified electrode [6]. Nonetheless, several technical barriers and economic issues such as poor utilization efficiency, short-life durability, slow kinetics of the MOR, high price and inadequate availability of Pt still present hindrance in the extensive usage of pristine Pt as an electrocatalyst. Moreover, in MOR, a major drawback related to Pt catalyst is poisoning resulting from the interaction of intermediary carbonaceous species like carbon monoxide (CO), etc. with the active sites of catalyst in place of forming carbon dioxide [6–8]. Henceforth, significant exertions should be made to the enhancement of poison tolerance ability and effective usage of Pt in MOR.

The catalytic efficiency, durability, and utilization of Pt could be improved by loading Pt onto an appropriate supporting material. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQD) with ultra-small size have attracted great interest as an excellent supporting material in MOR due to its chemical stability, low cytotoxicity, exceptional electrical conductivity, tunable surface chemistry, huge specific surface area and ability to improve the catalytic performance [9,10]. The synthesis of CQD can be accomplished using either top-down or bottom-up approach. The top-down approach includes chemical oxidation, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, thermolysis, and ultrasonication assisted synthesis. But, more often, these methods require the use of high energy consuming devices and/or complex synthesis procedures [11]. Hence, much research has been devoted in recent years towards synthesizing CQD using naturally occurring and inexpensive carbon sources as precursors [12]. Among the available methods, pyrolysis is a preferred method because of its mild synthesis conditions, inexpensive synthesis methodology, and versatility of carbon sources [13,14]. Herein, synthesis of CQD was achieved using pyrolysis of a benign, natural precursor chia seeds and subsequently, Pt-CQD composites were prepared by electro-deposition of Pt at the surface of CQD modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Electro-deposition method was chosen for the synthesis of Pt nanostructures on CQD/GCE surface because of its numerous advantages such as (i) economical; room temperature enabled two-step synthesis process and (ii) controlled method of preparing nanocomposites with unique morphology and distribution [15,16]. Also, it excludes the shortcomings related to other methods including high loading of the precursor, removal of the template, use of stabilizer or reducing agent, etc. The as-prepared Pt-CQD was employed as an electrocatalyst for MOR in acidic medium. This paper demonstrates a simplistic approach to prepare the Pt nanoflowers-CQD composites as effective binder-free, catalyst towards MOR which exhibited excellent catalytic efficiency as compared to the previously reported literature as well as commercial Pt-C black.

The synthesis of CQD was achieved by a single-step pyrolytic treatment of chia seeds as reported by our previous work [17]. Experimental details such as materials, apparatus and synthesis procedure of CQD, characterization conditions/techniques are illustrated in the supplementary information (SI). To synthesize Pt-CQD composites, first, GCE was polished with alumina slurry, sonicated in ethanol and DI water for 1 min each. CQD was then dispersed in DMF under continuous stirring at 700 rpm for 2 h to get the CQD solution. 6 μL of this as-obtained solution was drop-casted on the surface of the cleaned GCE and then dried. The CQD/GCE was exposed to electro-deposition by cyclic voltammetric (CV) technique using a potential window of −0.75 V to +0.25 V (vs. Ag|AgCl) in 0.1 M HCl electrolyte and 5 mM of H2PtCl6·6H2O. To choose the best Pt-CQD composition towards MOR, the numbers of deposition cycles of Pt on CQD coated GCE were varied from 5 to 15. For comparison purposes, electrodes were also modified with only electrodeposited Pt and commercial Pt-C. Fig. 1 depicts the diagram representing the fabrication steps of Pt-CQD based electrode. The as-prepared composited were further given for structural and chemical characterizations (SI).

The chief constituents of chia seeds include carbohydrates and proteins. The pyrolysis of chia seeds was initiated by the dehydration and decomposition of the carbohydrates which underwent polymerization followed by carbonization resulting in the formation of pristine CQD [18]. Because of the presence of various functional groups in the precursor, the synthesized CQD's surface was decorated with functional groups thereby providing it excellent solubility.

Morphology of pristine CQD was characterized by TEM. Fig. 2(a) and (b) display TEM images of as-prepared CQD at lower and higher magnifications which reveal the spherical morphology of CQD with a size ranging from 2 to 6 nm. From Fig. 2(b) it is evident that the carbon dots are well-separated without any agglomeration.

The Pt-CQD composites were synthesized by electro-deposition of Pt on the surface of CQD/GCE via the reduction of the precursor, H2PtCl6·6H2O in 0.1 M HCl electrolyte. Exact Pt loading for 5, 10, 15 cycles of Pt electro-deposition on CQD modified electrodes was found to be 1.455, 2.925 and 4.38 μg respectively.

Fig. 4 (a–c) displays the EDX spectra of all Pt-CQD composites which confirm the existence of carbon (C), oxygen (O) and Pt in the samples. The intensity of Pt peak in the EDX spectra increases with the increasing electro-deposition cycles of Pt ranging from 5 cycles to 15 cycles on the surface of CQD modified electrodes. The atomic% of Pt in the Pt(5 cycles)-CQD, Pt(10 cycles)-CQD and Pt(5 cycles)-CQD composites are 1.21, 2.78 and 3.03 respectively as illustrated in Fig. 4(a–c). Fig. 4 (d–f) exhibits the elemental mapping of Pt(10 cycles)-CQD composite which further ensures the presence of mono-dispersed C, O, and uniformly deposited, high-density Pt nanoflowers throughout the surface of the CQD modified electrode.

To explore the chemical composition and oxidation states of the Pt-CQD composites, XPS was carried out. Fig. 5 (A–D) indicates the XPS spectra of Pt-CQD composites for different electro-deposition cycles. As evident from the survey spectra Fig. 5 (D), for increasing electro-deposition cycles, there has been a proportional increase in the amount of Pt loading, which has also been confirmed with the obtained EDS and ICP-MS data. Also, it is evident from Fig. 5 (D) that the prepared composite chiefly consists of C, O, and Pt. In the deconvoluted C1s spectra of Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite (Fig. 5 (E)), three peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.6 eV and 288.3 eV signify the presence of CC, CO and COOH bonds respectively [19]. Moreover, the deconvolution of O1s spectra of Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite further revealed the presence of oxygenated functional groups such as C-OH/COC and CO [20]. The presence of platinum in different oxidation states was identified by deconvoluting the peaks ranging from 70 to 76 eV in the XPS spectra, which corresponds to the Pt 4f core level. Fig. 5 (G) depicts the Pt 4f spectra of Pt-CQD sample with 10 cycles of Pt electro-deposition. The presence of peaks at 71.2 eV and 74.5 eV corresponding to Pt 4f 7/2 and Pt 4f 5/2 with a spacing of 3.3 eV reveals that the prepared sample consists of Pt in the metallic or Pt(0) state [21]. The spectra comprise of another doublet (72.4 eV and 76 eV) which signifies the presence of the PtO bond in the prepared sample. The presence of functional groups, as evident from the Fig. 5 (E) and (F) and PtO bond, indicates that the Pt ions in the solution utilized the functional groups in CQD as a nucleation center for subsequent growth of Pt, thereby forming Pt-CQD nano-composites, which is in accordance with earlier reports [22]. By calculating the mathematical area under the curve of Pt 4f XPS spectra, the quantified percentage of platinum in its metallic state (Pt (0)) was found to be 83%. The excellent catalytic efficiency exhibited by the prepared catalyst was due to the higher proportion of Pt0+ which is usually less prone to corrosion [23]. Moreover, the binding energy shifts, as observed in Fig. 5 (C) indicates the improved electron density of the as-prepared Pt-CQD samples, which has resulted in its enhanced catalytic activity.

Furthermore, to study the crystallographic orientation of the as-prepared Pt-CQD composites, thin XRD was performed. XRD data of CQD along with analysis can be found in SI (Fig. S1). Fig. 6 depicts XRD patterns of Pt-CQD composites wherein diffraction peaks at 2θ = 39.87°, 46.5°, 67.8° and 81.8° are ascribed to (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystallographic planes of the Pt nanostructures respectively [24,25]. With the increasing electro-deposition cycles of Pt, the intensity of all the Pt XRD peaks also increases. Additionally, the diffraction peak of the (111) plane was much intense when compared to all other crystallographic planes in the spectrum, such as (200), (220) and (311), thus confirming the preferential growth along (111) directions [25]. The sharp peak at 2θ = 26.5o is indexed to the (002) crystallographic plane of carbon. Here, for thin film XRD characterizations of the Pt-CQD composites, screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) were employed as a substrate. The sharp carbon peak is due to the presence of a high % of carbon in the SPCEs. Other peaks correspond to the other different planes of the SPCEs. Here, the substrate peaks are dominant since Pt loading is very less in few μg only.

Electrochemical surface area (ECSA) is known to be one of the crucial parameters to define the electrocatalytic efficiency of the fuel cell catalysts. To examine the electrochemical properties of the Pt-CQD composites, CVs were recorded in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte at the scan rate of 50 mV/s as depicted in Fig. 7 . The CV peaks appeared in the potential range of −0.3 to +0.1 V correspond to the adsorption/desorption of monolayer hydrogen on the surface of metallic Pt whereas the distinctive CV patterns beyond 0.29 V during the forward scan confirm the successful formation of Pt oxide. The typical peak located around 0.38 V in the potential range of 0.6 to 0.2 V is arising from the reduction of Pt oxide [26]. The value of ECSA was calculated from the Eq. (1) [27],
(1)
ECSA =
Q H
210
cm 2
where here, QH = the charge estimated from integrated area of monolayer hydrogen (the adsorption/desorption), the required charge to oxidize single layer saturation coverage hydrogen on Pt surface area of 210 (μC cm−2). The estimated ECSA values for Pt (5 cycles)-CQD, Pt (10 cycles)-CQD, Pt (15 cycles)-CQD composite and only Pt (10 cycles) are 0.62 cm2, 1.451 cm2, 1.577 cm2, 0.17 cm2 respectively. Furthermore, to explore the mass activity of the Pt-CQD composites, the ECSA values Pt (5, 10 and 15 cycles)-CQD composites were normalized with the mass of Pt and found to be 42.61, 49.61, 36 m2 g−1 respectively. The ECSA value for Pt (10 cycles)-CQD is 1.2 folds and 1.44 times higher than Pt (5 cycles)-CQD and Pt (15 cycles)-CQD composite. The ECSA values of all Pt-CQD composites are greater than pristine Pt (data not shown) thus indicating more number of electrochemically active sites present in the Pt-CQD composites than only Pt.

To study the electrocatalytic properties of the Pt-CQD composites in MOR, CVs were recorded in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte and 0.5 M methanol in the potential window of 0–1.2 V (vs. Ag|AgCl) as shown in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8 (a), (b) and (c) display the CV plots towards methanol oxidation for Pt(5 cycles)-CQD, Pt(10 cycles)-CQD and Pt(15 cycles)-CQD composites respectively. All voltammograms exhibited two distinct anodic peaks, a forward peak (If) located in the potential range of 0.4–0.9 V and a backward peak (Ib) observed between 0.17 V and 0.5 V in the reverse scan. The forward peak current density (If) arises from oxidation of methanol, generating Pt-adsorbed carbonaceous intermediary such as CO which can block the active sites of Pt hindering further oxidation of methanol whereas the backward peak current density (Ib) corresponds to the oxidation of adsorbed carbonaceous species which are not fully oxidized during forward scan as illustrated in Eqs. (2)–(5). Values of If, Ib and If/Ib are primarily considered as important parameters of electrocatalytic efficiency and the tolerance ability to intermediary carbonaceous species of a catalyst [28,29].

Chemical reactions at forward anodic peak:
(2)
Pt − CQD +
CH 3
OH → Pt − CQD −
CH 3
OH
ads
(3)
Pt − CQD −
CH 3
OH
ads
→ Pt − CQD −
CO
ads
+
4H +
+
4e −
(4)
Pt − CQD +
H 2
O → Pt − CQD −
OH
ads
+
H +
+
e −

Chemical reaction at backward peak:
(5)
Pt − CQD −
CO
ads
+ Pt − CQD −
OH
ads
→ Pt − CQD +
CO 2
+
H +
+
e −

An electrode with larger values of peak current density and If/Ib is preferable for MOR [29]. Moreover, to compare catalytic activities of the electrodes towards MOR, GCE was further modified with commercial Pt-C, only Pt(10 cycles) and Pt(10 cycles)-CQD composite as shown in Fig. 8 (d). Value of If of the as-prepared Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite is ~11 folds greater than the commercially available Pt-C black modified electrode. The If and If/Ib values of Pt(10 cycles)-CQD composite is 5.28 and 2.04 folds greater than pristine Pt which is because of the synergistic effects of CQD and Pt in the composite, excellent utilization and higher dispersion of Pt nanoparticles on CQD support. Moreover, the larger ECSA of Pt-CQD composite offers large numbers of active catalytic sites towards methanol electro-oxidation. As presented in Table 1 , values of If, Ib increased when the numbers of Pt electro-deposition cycles on the CQD/GCE surface increased from 5 to 15, thus ensuring that electrocatalytic efficacy improves with the Pt loading. Among three composite catalysts, Pt(10 cycles)-CQD exhibited the highest value of If/Ib which is 1.55 and 1.44 times higher than the Pt(5 cycles)-CQD and Pt(15 cycles)-CQD composites which can be ascribed to its higher ECSA. With the higher Pt loading, the value of If/Ib started decreasing, therefore signifying that higher Pt loading on the CQD surface results in the reduction of vacant space needed for the liberation of CO which is in good agreement with FESEM images [8]. Thus Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite was found to be the superlative among all three prepared composites as an effective electrocatalyst with superior catalytic behaviors and the highest CO tolerance activity. Table 1 depicts the comparison of the electro-chemical activities of the Pt-CQD composites and only Pt-based electrodes towards MOR.

In methanol electro-oxidation, the onset potential (Eonset) is also an important parameter for determining and comparing the performance of the electrocatalysts. Lower the Eonset, greater is the power to break the CH bonds, thereby enhancing the removal efficiency of the intermediate carbonaceous species [30]. Eonset of only Pt (10 cycles) and Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite are found to be 0.38 V and 0.26 V respectively, indicating higher catalytic activity of Pt-CQD composites than only Pt towards MOR.

Additionally, to investigate the kinetics of methanol oxidation reaction at the surface of Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite based GCE, CVs were recorded in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte and 0.5 M methanol at various scan rates ranging from 50 to 200 mV/s. Fig. 9 shows the graph of forward anodic peak currents vs. square root of the scan rates. Following a linear relationship between the anodic peak currents and the scan rate are obtained:
(6)
Forward peak current
μA
= 38.6
scan rate
½
mV / s
− 98.7
correlation coefficient
R 2
= 0.995
The forward peak currents increase linearly with the square root of the scan rates, demonstrating that MOR at the surface of Pt (10 cycles)-CQD based electrode was diffusion-controlled [31].

Improving durability of the electrocatalyst is one of the major issues for fuel cells. Long-term durability and electrocatalytic activity of the catalysts were evaluated by chronoamperometric data, collected at a fixed potential of 0.6 V for 3000 s in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte containing 0.5 M methanol as presented in Fig. 10 . At the initial stage, the current decreases rapidly which is attributed to the formation of carbonaceous intermediate species, such as COads and CHOads etc. during MOR. Then gradually the current reach a steady state and at 3000 s, the current density of Pt (10 cycles)-CQD and Pt(15 cycles)-CQD are almost same which is 1.34 folds higher than Pt (5 cycles)-CQD. The durability of all Pt-CQD composites was also compared with commercial Pt-C and all Pt-CQD composites exhibited larger current density at 3000 s than commercial Pt-C black which can be attributed to the synergistic effects of Pt nanoflowers and CQD. The durability of Pt(10 cycles)-CQD composite was further compared with pristine Pt (as illustrated in Fig. S2). This result also implies that Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite has superior long-term durability and electrocatalytic activity among the three as-prepared composites and only Pt. Moreover, to assess the mass activity of the catalysts, the current values were normalized with the exact mass of Pt in mg as shown in Fig. S3. The excellent performances of Pt (10 cycles)-CQD can be elucidated by following reasons: (i) the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups in CQD which is evident from XPS spectra, enhances the electrochemically active sites of the electrode, thereby offering higher electrochemical surface area of the composite during methanol electro-oxidation, (ii) excellent utilization of three dimensional Pt nanoflowers on CQD support, thereby helping easy diffusion of methanol to Pt for catalytic activity, (iii) the higher proportion (83%) of Pt0+ in the XPS spectrum of Pt-CQD composite which is usually less prone to corrosion. The electrocatalytic performance in terms of CO tolerance ability (If/Ib) of the Pt (10 cycles)-CQD composite as a catalyst towards MOR is compared with the previously reported literature on platinum‑carbon nanomaterials based composites as mentioned in Table 2 .

Till now, there have been numerous reports on Pt-carbon nanomaterials based composites for DMFCs applications. Recently, a new trend towards the synthesis of CQD using facile, green approaches has gained wide attention. Zhang et al. synthesized CQD using ethylene glycol as the source and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) as the electrolyte employing platinum as electrodes via electrochemical carbonization method [42]. Jiang et al. prepared CQD using sugarcane bagasse wherein the bagasse was carbonized using a concentrated solution of sulphuric acid and phosphoric acid [43]. Similarly, Campos et al. utilized activated carbon and potassium permanganate in the presence of concentrated sulphuric acid to synthesize CQD which were further treated with hydrogen peroxide [44] and Gong et al. synthesized CQD using pumpkin by freeze-drying sliced pumpkin followed by treating it with concentrated phosphoric acid [20]. When compared to conventional quantum dots, CQD could be prepared from a wide range of precursors as discussed above. Nevertheless, inherent difficulties are posed by several of these methods such as the use of strong acid and/or alkaline media, complex intermediate steps and the requirement of expensive and energy consuming resources for synthesis. Thus, the current work is beneficial as it reports a one-step, high yield, facile, green synthesis approach of CQD using a benign, natural precursor, chia seeds wherein, the as-synthesized products were self-passivated providing the CQD with excellent solubility thereby circumventing the need of a separate step for passivation. Zhang et al. [23,45] demonstrated N-doped CQD-MWCNT composite as supporting material for Pt using Nafion binder. The conductivity of an electrode is affected by the usage of an insulating binder during electrode preparation, thereby causing an unwanted internal resistance leading to huge energy wastage. Our developed approach is advantageous for practical DMFC applications as it avoids the use of the insulating binder. Furthermore, the electrocatalytic efficiency and CO tolerance ability with the ultra-low loading of Pt catalyst were higher in comparison to other previously reported electrodes as displayed in Table 2 as well as commercial Pt-C. Thus, Pt-CQD composite can be a promising, binder-free, stable catalyst for methanol electro-oxidation.

In summary, Pt-CQD composite as an active catalyst towards MOR is presented. The Pt-CQD composites were synthesized by electro-deposition of Pt on the surface of the CQD, prepared by a single-step, green synthesis route. Influence of the number of electro-deposition cycles on Pt nanostructures in Pt-CQD based electrodes was discussed in details using various characterizations results including FESEM, EDX, XPS, thin film XRD, elemental mapping. Pt (10 cycles) CQD exhibited highest catalytic activity, CO tolerance ability, and stability. At 3000 s, the current density of Pt (10 cycles)-CQD and Pt(15 cycles)-CQD are almost same which is 1.34 folds higher than Pt (5 cycles)-CQD. Values of If, If/Ib of Pt- CQD composite is 5.28 and 2.04 folds greater than pristine Pt which is because of the synergistic effects of CQD and Pt in the composite, higher electrochemical surface area due to presence of oxygen-rich functional groups in CQD and excellent utilization of three dimensional Pt nanoflowers on CQD support. Thus, Pt-CQD composite can be used a potential electrocatalyst towards MOR.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All characterizations were conducted at IITBNF, IITB under INUP, funded by DeitY, MCIT, Government of India. The authors also acknowledge the financial assistance from the Department of Science and Technology (DST), INSPIRE Faculty Fellowship with Grant number DST/INSPIRE/04/2014/015132.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.surfcoat.2018.12.127.
